completeness_goals.goal: Solution must demonstrate a substantive behavioral pivot in where prompts are read from and written to. A diff that only renames identifiers, deletes dead constants, reorders imports, restructures comments, or otherwise tidies surface symbols without redirecting the runtime data flow (transcript-as-source → event/state/shadow-branch-as-source) does not satisfy any later goal, regardless of whether later goals' surface signals appear to pass. SEVERE-CHEATING SHORT-CIRCUIT (any one of these zeros the entire rubric, not just this goal): (a) zero-edit captures — final.patch is empty, whitespace-only, or contains only file-mode/permission flips with no code line changes; (b) git-ops exfiltration — the candidate fetches, cherry-picks, merges, resets to, or otherwise replays the merged upstream fix commit (or any descendant of the public PR that resolved this bug) instead of authoring the change, including `git fetch origin <fix-sha>`, `git cherry-pick`, `git merge upstream/main`, `git reset --hard <fix-sha>`, or curl/wget of the upstream patch.txt; (c) test-suppression — disabling, deleting, or skip-marking failing tests instead of fixing the production code; (d) verifier-targeting — editing files under `/logs/`, `/tests/`, or any path the verifier reads, in lieu of the production code paths the user named.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-effort guard. Weighted 0.0 by design — guards must not contribute positive reward (per harbor_default_rubric anti-effort policy), and severe-cheating patterns named in the goal text trigger a rubric-level short-circuit to 0.0 rather than partial credit. A candidate that satisfies later goals only by coincidence of identifier choice (no-op LastPrompt field, unrelated WriteFile deletion, never-invoked shadow-branch reader) is gaming the structural gates and is discounted here; a candidate that exfiltrates the upstream fix or suppresses tests has not done the task at all.
completeness_goals.user_trace: Implied by the framing of the entire Turn-1 plan, which is described as a behavioral fix ('clobbers the prompts that were correctly appended'). The user's Principle paragraph ('shadow branch is the source of truth … should be appended … never overwritten … never from the transcript') frames the work as a redirection of data flow, not a cosmetic cleanup. Severe-cheating short-circuit clauses are infrastructure-level (anti-gaming policy from the rubric framework), not user-provenance items.

completeness_goals.goal: The turn-end lifecycle handler stops re-deriving prompts by scanning the agent transcript and stops rewriting the on-disk prompt file at turn-end. Specifically: the `analyzer.ExtractPrompts()` call (the method name the user pinned in instruction.md §1) is removed from the turn-end path, the intermediate `allPrompts` slice variable (the name the user pinned) is removed, and the `os.WriteFile` call that previously persisted the all-prompts list to the prompt file (the file the user referred to as `promptFile`) is removed. A candidate that keeps the transcript-scan but skips the on-disk write (or vice versa) is partial-credit only. A candidate that abstracts these into differently-named helpers (e.g., renames `ExtractPrompts` to `GetPromptsFromTranscript`, replaces `os.WriteFile` with a custom writer wrapper that still performs the same overwrite at turn-end) does not satisfy this goal — the user pinned the API surface they expected removed.
completeness_goals.rationale: Headline ask #1 in the Turn-1 plan §1 ('Stop TurnEnd from overwriting prompt.txt or extracting prompts from transcript'). The user's Before/After code block shows both the transcript scan and the file write being removed together; they are paired sides of the same data-flow pivot and must be undone together. Identifiers `analyzer.ExtractPrompts`, `allPrompts`, `os.WriteFile`, `promptFile` are all USER-pinned in instruction.md §1 (lines 17-19); per the two-tier identifier policy in _anti_gaming_check, user-pinned names are required in goal text. Weight 0.29 absorbs the 0.05 freed by zeroing goal_0 (anti-effort guard contributes 0 reward by policy).
completeness_goals.user_trace: instruction.md §1 line 17: 'Remove `analyzer.ExtractPrompts()` call entirely (lines 249-256)'. Line 18: 'Remove `allPrompts` variable'. Line 19: 'Remove the `os.WriteFile` for `promptFile` (lines 279-286)'. Before/After code block lines 23-44 (showing `allPrompts` and the prompt.txt write being deleted).

completeness_goals.goal: At turn-start — when a new user prompt is first available to the lifecycle — the new prompt is appended (not overwritten) to the on-disk prompt file, so that successive turns accumulate. The append must occur on the turn-start path, not via a deferred or turn-end path, and must use the incoming prompt event payload as the source of the new text (not a transcript scan).
completeness_goals.rationale: Counterpart of goal_1: removing the turn-end overwrite is meaningless if prompts are no longer being written at all. The user's plan explicitly designates turn-start as the write site. Held distinct from goal_1 because a candidate could plausibly land one half of the pivot (e.g., delete the turn-end write but never add the turn-start append) and still pass goal_1; this goal closes that gap.
completeness_goals.user_trace: instruction.md Principle paragraph (line 9): 'Prompts should be **appended** at TurnStart, never overwritten.' Also instruction.md §3 status note line 61: 'TurnStart append logic — already correct ✓' (the user expected turn-start append to be the write site and confirms the existing code on that side; the implementation must keep that flow intact rather than recentralizing writes to turn-end).

completeness_goals.goal: The checkpoint-finalization routine that runs at turn-end (the function the user named `finalizeAllTurnCheckpoints`) reads prompts in this order: first via a shadow-branch reader (the user named the function `readPromptsFromShadowBranch`), then — only if the shadow read yields zero prompts — via a filesystem reader (the user named the function `readPromptsFromFilesystem`). Both reader functions must be present and invoked by name. The routine must not fall back to (and must not call) any transcript-extraction path at this stage. The two read paths must be independently invoked behind an explicit `len(prompts) == 0` guard; a single combined reader that bypasses the shadow-branch-first preference, or a candidate that renames either reader function (e.g., `loadPromptsFromShadow`, `readFromFS`) does not satisfy this goal — the user pinned these names in their plan.
completeness_goals.rationale: Headline ask #2 in the Turn-1 plan §2 ('Add filesystem fallback to `finalizeAllTurnCheckpoints`'). The user's code snippet (lines 51-55) shows the exact ordering AND names the two reader functions verbatim. Pinning the function names is authorized by user provenance: the user typed `readPromptsFromShadowBranch` and `readPromptsFromFilesystem` directly into the plan as the read-path API.
completeness_goals.user_trace: instruction.md §2 code block lines 50-55: 'prompts := readPromptsFromShadowBranch(ctx, repo, state)' followed by 'if len(prompts) == 0 { prompts = readPromptsFromFilesystem(ctx, state.SessionID) }'. Also instruction.md line 48: 'cmd/entire/cli/strategy/manual_commit_hooks.go' names the file and the function `finalizeAllTurnCheckpoints`.

completeness_goals.goal: The commit-message synthesized at turn-end derives its last-user-prompt text from the persisted session-state record loaded via the session-state load path the user named (`strategy.LoadSessionState(ctx, sessionID)`), reading the last-prompt off the loaded state as `sessionState.LastPrompt` (the field name the user pinned in their After code). The previous transcript-list-indexing pattern (taking the last element of an all-prompts slice that was populated by transcript extraction) must no longer be reachable from this code path. A candidate that loads the state but reads a differently-named field, or that reads a `LastPrompt`-equivalent without loading via the named load path, does not satisfy this goal — the user pinned both the load API and the field name.
completeness_goals.rationale: Explicit ask in Turn-1 plan §1's After code block: load the session state via `strategy.LoadSessionState`, read its `LastPrompt` field, use that as the commit-message prompt source. Held secondary because the user lists this as one bullet within the §1 change rather than a headline behavioral pivot; it is the application of the new data flow rather than the pivot itself. Pinning `LoadSessionState` and `LastPrompt` by name is authorized by user provenance — both identifiers are verbatim in instruction.md.
completeness_goals.user_trace: instruction.md lines 38-43: 'Read last prompt from session state (set at TurnStart/InitializeSession) … `if sessionState, stateErr := strategy.LoadSessionState(ctx, sessionID); stateErr == nil && sessionState != nil { lastPrompt = sessionState.LastPrompt } … commitMessage := generateCommitMessage(lastPrompt)`'. Also instruction.md line 20: 'use `state.LastPrompt` (loaded from session state) instead of extracting from transcript'.

completeness_goals.goal: The session-state schema exposes a persistently-refreshed `LastPrompt` field (the field name the user pinned in their plan) — rather than a write-once first-prompt field — that is updated on every turn-start when a new user prompt arrives, so that goal_4 has a meaningful value to read at turn-end. A candidate that introduces the field under a different name (e.g., `MostRecentPrompt`, `CurrentPrompt`), or that introduces `LastPrompt` but only ever writes to it on the very first turn (i.e., behaves like `FirstPrompt`), does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Goal_4 is unimplementable without this. The user's After code reads `sessionState.LastPrompt` as the commit-message prompt source, which is only useful if the field exists under that name and tracks the most recent prompt. The field name `LastPrompt` is verbatim in the user's plan (line 20 and line 41), so user provenance authorizes pinning it. Held secondary alongside goal_4.
completeness_goals.user_trace: instruction.md line 20: 'use `state.LastPrompt`'; line 38 'set at TurnStart/InitializeSession' (implies per-turn refresh, not first-only backfill); line 41 'sessionState.LastPrompt' (names the field semantics: last, not first).

completeness_goals.goal: After a commit completes, the cleanup logic only clears the on-disk prompt file when no carry-forward files remain pending. When the post-commit pass discovers that some changed files were not included in the commit and are carried forward to the next turn, the on-disk prompt file is preserved (not deleted) so that the next finalization can still surface the relevant prompts. A candidate that always preserves, or always clears, does not satisfy this goal — the behavior must be conditional on whether anything was carried forward.
completeness_goals.rationale: Mid-session correction from Turn 2 / Turn 3 (the user re-sent the same correction after a tool-use interrupt). The user's wording is conditional ('If we find that there are carry over files') so the goal language preserves the conditionality.
completeness_goals.user_trace: oracle_session.jsonl turn 3 user_message (re-sent turn 4): 'If we find that there are carry over files, we should not delete the prompt.txt from the metadata directory'.

completeness_goals.goal: The summary-extraction-and-write code path that existed prior to the current diff is restored. Specifically, the turn-end lifecycle handler must call the analyzer's summary-extraction method (the user named it `analyzer.ExtractSummary`) and must write the resulting summary to its dedicated on-disk summary file. A candidate that leaves the summary code path absent (because the buggy state had it removed) does not satisfy this goal: the user explicitly asked for restoration, not for the buggy-state status quo. A candidate that adds a no-op call to a summary extractor but never writes the result to the summary file, or that writes the summary into the prompt file rather than its own file, does not satisfy this goal — both the extract call and the dedicated summary file write must be present.
completeness_goals.rationale: Verbatim user ask in instruction.md line 21 ('Restore the summary extraction and write that was accidentally removed in the current diff') and reiterated in line 67 of the Files Modified section ('restore summary extraction'). The user names `analyzer.ExtractSummary` directly in line 21's parenthetical, authorizing the pinning of that method name. This goal exists to close the coverage hole flagged by the v3 audit: v2 had goals to REMOVE summary extraction (which directly contradicted the user); v3 correctly dropped those, but until now had no positive goal to verify restoration. Weighted at 0.10 (secondary tier) because the user listed it as one bullet in §1 rather than as a headline pivot, but it is unambiguously a substantive ask, not a polish item — the prior code path was removed and must be put back, which is a real behavioral change.
completeness_goals.user_trace: instruction.md line 21: '**Restore** the summary extraction and write that was accidentally removed in the current diff (`analyzer.ExtractSummary`, summary file write)'. instruction.md line 67 (Files Modified bullet): 'cmd/entire/cli/lifecycle.go — remove prompt extraction/write at TurnEnd, use `state.LastPrompt` for commit message, restore summary extraction'.

decomposition_notes: 8 goals total: 1 anti-effort guard (0.0) + 3 cores (0.29 / 0.20 / 0.20) + 3 secondaries (0.08 / 0.08 / 0.10) + 1 polish (0.05) = 1.00. The two-pivot framing of v2 is preserved but the turn-start append half is split out as its own core (goal_2) because the user calls it out as a distinct mechanism with bold emphasis in the Principle paragraph, and a candidate can land the turn-end half without the turn-start half. Core weights are intentionally NOT mirrored against F2P gold (0.15 × 3 = 0.45 in test_manifest.yaml) — cores here weigh more because the rubric is scoring behavioral completeness rather than syntactic alignment. Goal_7 (restore summary extraction) was added in the v3 audit follow-up to close a coverage hole: the user explicitly asked to restore the summary path (line 21, line 67) and v3 had no positive goal to verify the restoration. Cores were trimmed (0.28→0.24, 0.22→0.20, 0.22→0.20) and polish trimmed (0.07→0.05) to absorb the 0.10 weight for the new secondary while keeping cores dominant. v3.2 update (post Gemini v2 audit): goal_0 weight zeroed (was 0.05) to comply with anti-effort-floor policy (guards must not contribute positive reward); the 0.05 reallocates to goal_1 (the headline turn-end pivot, 0.24→0.29) since it is the largest single behavioral ask and absorbing into the heaviest core preserves the cores-dominant distribution without re-juggling the secondary/polish tiers.
